children.name: Phylloscopus
children.description: Leaf warblers — small, active, largely green or yellow-green warblers that glean insects from foliage with remarkable agility. The willow warbler (lövsångare) is the single most numerous summer bird in Sweden by individual count, estimated at over 40 million breeding pairs across Scandinavia and northern Europe. Its song — a cascading, liquid descent of sweet notes — is arguably the defining sound of Swedish spring and early summer, heard in every birch woodland and willow scrub from Skåne to the Arctic fells. Despite weighing just 9 grams, the willow warbler completes an annual round trip of 10,000–14,000 km to sub-Saharan Africa and back. The closely similar chiffchaff is distinguished almost entirely by its onomatopoeic song — 'chiff-chaff-chiff-chaff' repeated endlessly — and has been expanding its breeding range northward into Sweden with warming temperatures.

children.name: Phylloscopus trochilus
children.description: The lövsångare — Sweden's most numerous summer bird. An estimated 40+ million pairs breed across Scandinavia. Its cascading, descending song is the soundtrack of Swedish spring. Weighs 9 g but migrates to Africa and back annually. Arrives late April; song falls silent by July when breeding ends.

children.name: Phylloscopus collybita
children.description: The gransångare — one of Sweden's earliest spring arrivals. Its repetitive 'chiff-chaff' song is unmistakeable and often heard before the bird is seen. Increasingly overwintering in southern Sweden near water, especially in mild years. Almost identical to willow warbler but with dark legs.

children.name: Phylloscopus sibilatrix
children.description: The largest and most vividly coloured leaf warbler in Europe — yellow-green above, brilliant white below with a yellow supercilium. Produces two distinct songs: a silvery trill (like a spinning coin) and a series of plaintive 'piu' notes. A bird of mature deciduous woodland with minimal understory. Declining in Scandinavia.

children.name: Phylloscopus borealis
children.description: Breeds in mountain birch forest in northern Sweden — the northernmost leaf warbler in Europe. Migrates to Southeast Asia for winter, one of the longest migrations of any European passerine. A prize sighting at Swedish mountain birch woodland in summer.

children.name: Sylvia
children.description: Sylvia warblers — robust, skulking warblers of dense scrub and woodland edge. The blackcap (svarthätta) is one of Sweden's finest songsters — its rich, melodious outpouring from deep within a hedgerow or garden is ranked alongside the nightingale and blackbird. Blackcaps have undergone a remarkable evolutionary shift in recent decades: a new migratory population has established that winters in Britain rather than Africa, facilitated by garden bird feeders. These 'British wintering' blackcaps now return to breeding grounds in central Europe earlier than their Africa-wintering counterparts, and the two populations increasingly breed separately. The common whitethroat (törnsångare) delivers its scratchy, exuberant song from a shrub top with a characteristic dancing delivery.

children.name: Sylvia atricapilla
children.description: The svarthätta — one of Sweden's finest singers. Black cap (male) or rusty-brown cap (female). Increasingly common in Sweden and arriving earlier each decade. Now regularly overwinters in southernmost Sweden, attracted by garden feeding stations.

children.name: Sylvia borin
children.description: Perhaps the least distinguished-looking passerine in Europe — completely uniform warm brown — but produces a rich, continuous song that rivals the blackcap's. A summer visitor to Swedish woodland edge and scrub. Its plainness is so extreme that it is sometimes called the most nondescript bird in Europe.

children.name: Sylvia communis
children.description: The törnsångare — a scratchy, exuberant singer of Swedish hedgerows, forest edges, and scrubby patches. Males have a white throat and grey head. Delivers its song from a bramble top with characteristic bouncy display flights. A common summer visitor across all of southern Sweden.
